title: Long-range nonstabilizerness of topologically encoded states from mutual information
authors: David Aram Korbany, Tyler D. Ellison, David T. Stephen, Lorenzo Piroli
abstract: We study long-range nonstabilizerness (LRN), namely the obstruction to remove nonstabilizerness with shallow-depth local quantum circuits. In one-dimensional settings, the mutual information between disconnected spatial regions has proven to be a powerful tool to diagnose LRN. In this work, we focus on encoded states of two-dimensional topologically-ordered systems, and explore the ability of the mutual information to serve as a diagnostic of LRN. Focusing on the concrete setting of lattice models defined on a torus, we show that information about LRN can be gained from the analysis of the mutual information between non-overlapping regions containing non-contractible loops, and of the change of such mutual information under modular real-space transformations. We exemplify this idea in the toric code and the non-abelian string-net model with doubled Fibonacci topological order. In the former case, we show that the mutual information provides a full classification, certifying LRN for all encoded non-stabilizer states. In the latter case, instead, our approach does not lead to a full classification, as it detects LRN for all states except from a finite subset with special transformation properties under the modular group. Finally, we discuss how our results on LRN constrain the logical gates that can be implemented fault-tolerantly on the torus.
summary: The paper investigates how mutual information between disjoint regions on a torus can detect long-range nonstabilizerness in topological phases. It provides a complete classification for the Toric Code and shows that while the method is powerful, it has limitations in non-abelian systems. The results also impose fundamental constraints on which logical gates can be implemented fault-tolerantly.
main_problem: The paper aims to characterize and detect long-range nonstabilizerness (LRN) in topologically encoded states in 2D, specifically determining if mutual information can serve as a reliable diagnostic tool.
main_result: The authors prove that for the Z2 toric code, non-integer mutual information between non-overlapping regions containing non-contractible loops provides a full classification of LRN, whereas for non-abelian Fibonacci models, the method detects LRN for all but a finite subset of states.
method: The study uses information-theoretic analysis of mutual information, modular transformations (S and T matrices) of the torus, and entropic uncertainty relations (Maassen-Uffink bound) to relate anyon properties to LRN.
model_or_system: The research focuses on 2D topologically ordered systems defined on a torus, specifically the Z2 Toric Code and non-abelian string-net models like the doubled Fibonacci topological order.
key_observables: Mutual information (I), von Neumann entropy, and anyon labels/flux.
important_parameters: Shallow-depth local quantum circuits (depth O(polylog(L))), thermodynamic limit (N -> infinity), and modular group transformations.
main_assumptions: The system is a regular 2D lattice; regions are sufficiently distant and large enough to encompass Wilson loop operators; the study focuses on minimal-entropy states.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows Toric Code operators and non-contractible cycles; Figure 2 illustrates spatial regions A and B; Figure 3 shows Wilson loop operator mappings; Figure 4 depicts the string-net lattice; Figure 5 shows a Dehn twist via lattice shear; Figure 6 shows a pictorial derivation of trace equations.
paper_structure: The paper introduces LRN and the problem of 2D detection, establishes a theorem for LRN detection using mutual information, applies this to the Z2 Toric Code for full classification, extends the analysis to non-abelian string-net models, and concludes with implications for fault-tolerant logical gates.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to many-body dynamics and quantum information as it provides a new information-theoretic tool to detect non-stabilizer resources in topological phases, which is crucial for understanding the limits of fault-tolerant quantum computing.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 4. Pictorial representation of the string-net lattice. The figure also shows the supports of the vertex, plaquette, and string operators.

Figure 5. Dehn twist on the torus implemented by a shear transformation (green arrows), followed by a local geometric deformation to restore the lattice. The coordinate grid rep- resents the connectivity of the lattice. Note that the shear transformation is a permutation where in the n-th column the vertices are shifted by n steps.
Figure 6. Pictorial derivation of Eq. (56). We only show the “ket” layer. The horizontal double lines (colored) indicate a trace over ˜C on the left, and over C in the middle pannel.
